Reflexive Causal Inference · Decision Intelligence

SHARP

The estimate that survives its own success — a fixed point you can hold in a market that keeps moving.

Your causal estimate was correct. Then you shipped it, the competitor answered, and the demand curve you measured stopped existing. This is the Lucas Critique, in production. Here is the mathematics of it — and an agentic controller that prices the game instead of the snapshot.

Python framework peer-review-ready paper reproducible benchmark

A framework, a peer-review-ready paper, and a fully reproducible benchmark — released together, and written to invite collaboration.

1 · The $1.5B forecast that kept believing in 2021 2 · The trap: a correct estimate, matched in 72 hours 3 · Structural narcissism: when your instrument turns endogenous 4 · The Reflexive Operator and its fixed point 5 · The Reflexivity Gap: what the snapshot costs 6 · One number: the reflexivity spectral radius 7 · The agentic answer: sense → detect → reason → act 8 · Does it work? The benchmark 9 · From one price to a portfolio of decisions 10 · Does this happen in the real world? A pharma case 11 · Run it yourself 12 · Honest novelty & limits

There is a particular kind of failure that only happens to teams good enough to have shipped something real. You did the hard part. You fought through the endogeneity, you found instruments, you cross-fitted the nuisance functions, you got a clean elasticity with a tight confidence interval. You put it into the pricing engine. For one quarter, it was the best decision you ever made.

Then it wasn’t.

This essay is about why that happens with a kind of grim inevitability, why it is not a modelling bug you can fix with more data, and what an agent has to do instead. The framework is called SHARP — Strategic-Hedged Adaptive Re-estimation for Pricing. Everything in it is open and reproducible; the code and paper are at the end.

01
The $1.5B forecast that kept believing in 2021

Start with the simplest version, the one that needs no game theory at all. A consumer-goods demand model — a well-tuned ARIMA — was trained through the 2021 boom. It learned the boom. When 2022 arrived and demand reverted, the model kept extrapolating the upward momentum it had internalised. Procurement believed it. The result was roughly $1.5 billion in markdowns on inventory that was ordered for a world that had already ended.

The model kept predicting 2021. The business paid for it in 2022.

The lesson usually drawn is “watch for regime shifts.” True, but too weak. The ARIMA’s sin was passive: it assumed tomorrow resembles yesterday and got caught by an exogenous break. The far more dangerous version is when the break is endogenous — when the very act of deploying your model is what ends the world it was trained on. That is where causal inference, the discipline we reach for precisely because it promises stability under intervention, turns on us.

02
The trap: a correct estimate, matched in 72 hours

Here is the scene, and if you have shipped pricing you have lived some version of it. You estimate an own-price elasticity with a two-sided instrumental-variables / double-ML pipeline. It is genuinely correct: unbiased, well-identified, validated. You set the profit-maximising price it implies. And then:

I shipped the IV-DML elasticity into production. Then my competitor matched in 72 hours. The estimate was correct for the equilibrium it measured — and lethal for the one it created.

Nothing about your econometrics was wrong. The elasticity you measured was the true elasticity of the market as it stood. But price is not a treatment you apply to a passive population of buyers. It is a move in a game, observed by players who move back. The moment your competitor re-prices, the cross-price term that was dormant in your data wakes up, the demand curve rotates, and your carefully identified coefficient is now describing a counterfactual world that no longer exists.

Economists have a name for the general phenomenon: the Lucas Critique. A relationship estimated under one policy regime cannot be assumed to survive a change in the policy rule, because the agents generating the data optimise against the rule. Lucas aimed it at macro-econometric models used for policy. It applies with full force, and much faster, to a pricing model used in a competitive market. What takes a central bank a business cycle takes your competitor a long weekend.

03
Structural narcissism: when your instrument turns endogenous

Let us be precise about the mechanism, because precision is what lets us fix it. Write log-demand for our product as

$$\log q \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \varepsilon_{\text{own}}\,\log a \;+\; \varepsilon_{\text{cross}}\,\log c \;+\; \gamma\, x \;+\; u,$$

where \(a\) is our price, \(c\) the competitor’s, \(x\) observed market state, and \(u\) a latent demand shock. Our price is endogenous — historically we moved it in response to \(u\) — so we instrument it with a cost shifter \(z_a\) that is excluded from demand. In a static market that exclusion holds and IV recovers \(\varepsilon_{\text{own}}\) cleanly.

Now the competitor becomes strategic: they best-respond to our price, \(\log c = c_0 + \kappa \log a_{t-L}\) with gain \(\kappa\) and latency \(L\). Trace the path of our instrument now:

$$z_a \;\longrightarrow\; a \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{c}_{\text{competitor reacts}} \;\longrightarrow\; q.$$

Our instrument now influences demand through the competitor’s response. The exclusion restriction — the one assumption that made the whole estimate causal — is broken by our own success. The instrument has become endogenous. This is the structural narcissism at the heart of the problem: the estimator assumes the world does not look back at it, and in a strategic market the world does nothing but look back.

A causal estimate is a photograph of a market that has already agreed to hold still. Strategic markets never agreed to that.

The decision-relevant quantity is not the partial elasticity \(\varepsilon_{\text{own}}\) but the equilibrium (total) elasticity that folds in the competitor’s reaction,

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \varepsilon_{\text{own}} + \varepsilon_{\text{cross}}\,\frac{d\log c}{d\log a} \;=\; \varepsilon_{\text{own}} + \varepsilon_{\text{cross}}\,\kappa .$$

In a static window \(d\log c/d\log a = 0\), so the two coincide and the snapshot looks fine. The instant the competitor starts responding, \(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}\) diverges from \(\varepsilon_{\text{own}}\), and a firm still pricing on \(\varepsilon_{\text{own}}\) is optimising against a demand curve it personally destroyed. You did not measure the elasticity. You measured the absence of a response.

04
The Reflexive Operator and its fixed point

To fix a moving target you first have to write down its motion. Define the Reflexive Operator \(\mathcal{T}\) as the full loop from belief to belief:

The operator θ → optimise → a*(θ) → competitor BR → c = BR(a*) → data → re-estimate → θ′

You hold a belief \(\theta\) about the world. You optimise against it and deploy \(a^*(\theta)\). The market — competitor included — responds, generating data. You re-estimate, obtaining \(\theta' = \mathcal{T}(\theta)\).

A belief that is still true after you have acted on it is a fixed point of this operator. We call it a Reflexive Causal Equilibrium (RCE):

$$\theta^\star \;=\; \mathcal{T}(\theta^\star).$$

The snapshot estimate is, in general, not a fixed point. \(\varepsilon_{\text{own}}\) is an RCE only when \(\varepsilon_{\text{cross}}\,\kappa = 0\) — a static market. Otherwise, iterating the operator either contracts toward the RCE or spirals away from it, and which one you get is not a matter of taste. It is governed by the slope of the loop.

05
The Reflexivity Gap: what the snapshot costs

The operator tells you whether you converge. The next object tells you what it costs you to be wrong in the meantime. Define the Reflexivity Gap as the profit difference between the policy you chose (optimal for the world you measured) and the RCE policy (optimal for the world you caused), both evaluated in the world you actually caused:

$$\mathcal{G} \;=\; \pi\!\left(a^*(\theta^\star)\,\middle|\,\text{induced world}\right)\;-\;\pi\!\left(a^*(\theta_{\text{snapshot}})\,\middle|\,\text{induced world}\right)\;\ge\;0.$$

It is exactly the money you leave on the table by optimising a photograph. With positive strategic complementarity, the competitor’s matching makes your effective demand less elastic than the snapshot implies, so the snapshot chronically under-prices — a subtle, self-inflicted bleed rather than a dramatic crash.

06
One number: the reflexivity spectral radius

Whether you should trust a point estimate at all comes down to a single diagnostic — the modulus of the loop’s linearisation, the reflexivity spectral radius:

$$\rho_{R} \;=\; \Big|\underbrace{\tfrac{d\,\mathrm{BR}}{d a}}_{\text{competitor reacts}}\Big|\cdot\Big|\underbrace{\tfrac{d a^*}{d \theta}}_{\text{you re-optimise}}\Big|\cdot\Big|\underbrace{\tfrac{d \hat\theta}{d a}}_{\text{you re-estimate}}\Big|.$$
  • \(\rho_R < 1\) — ship and re-estimate. The loop is a contraction. A rolling causal estimate chases the moving equilibrium and catches it. Point estimates are safe if you keep refreshing them.
  • \(\rho_R \ge 1\) — ship and hedge. The loop is expansive. Iterating a point estimate diverges; precision becomes a liability. The correct move is to stop optimising a point and optimise under an ambiguity set — distributionally robust, on purpose.

Crucially, \(\rho_R\) is estimable from the same pipeline that produces the elasticity — it is a property of the market you can measure, not a philosophical stance you have to adopt. That is what makes it usable by an agent.

07
The agentic answer: sense → detect → reason → act

None of this is actionable as a static report, because the whole problem is that the world will not hold still for a report. It is actionable as a control loop. This is where an agent earns its keep: not by being a smarter one-shot estimator, but by continuously re-running the entire estimate → diagnose → decide cycle and changing its own decision rule when the regime changes underneath it.

The SHARP controller runs four steps every period:

flowchart TD A["SENSE
rolling two-sided IV-DML
on a fresh window
+ fit competitor best-response κ"] --> B{"DETECT
CUSUM on structural residuals
first-stage F floor
is the instrument still alive?"} B -->|"instrument dead
(F < floor)"| E["ACT · HEDGE
distributionally-robust price
over an ambiguity set of κ, ε_cross"] B -->|"alive"| C{"REASON
reflexivity proxy
ρ̂ = |κ|·|ε_cross /(1+ε_own)|"} C -->|"ρ̂ < ρ* · static"| D["ACT · SNAPSHOT
price vs current competitor"] C -->|"ρ̂ ≥ ρ* · strategic"| F["ACT · REFLEXIVE
Stackelberg price:
c = BR(a) folded in"] style A fill:#eef1f7,stroke:#2b3a67,color:#22212b style B fill:#fbfaf5,stroke:#b98a25,color:#22212b style C fill:#fbfaf5,stroke:#b98a25,color:#22212b style D fill:#eef1f7,stroke:#5b6fa6,color:#22212b style F fill:#e7efeec,stroke:#2f7d78,color:#22212b style E fill:#fbeee7,stroke:#cf6a45,color:#22212b

The agent is not choosing a price. It is choosing which theory of the market to price against, and revising that choice as evidence arrives. In the static regime it behaves like the naive firm — and should, because there the snapshot is the RCE. When it detects the competitor waking up, it switches to a Stackelberg objective that prices the game. When identification genuinely collapses, it refuses to trust a point and hedges. You can watch it make these switches:

08
Does it work? The benchmark

A framework that cannot lose is not saying anything. So the benchmark is adversarial to SHARP in the only way that matters: the naive agent is not a strawman. It ships a genuinely correct IV estimate of the static elasticity, and in the static regime it is optimal. Both agents warm up identically. The competitor turns strategic at week 52. We compare cumulative profit across twelve random seeds.

MetricNaive snapshotSHARPDifference
Seeds won (of 12)111—
Cumulative profit, post-warmup1708 ± 2221941 ± 223+14.1%
Profit uplift, pre-break—−0.3%no premium paid
Profit uplift, post-break—+17.2%where it counts
Recovered \(\hat\varepsilon_{\text{own}}\)−2.31 ± 0.33structuraltrue = −2.20

Two features matter more than the headline number. First, the pre-break premium is essentially zero (−0.3%): SHARP’s machinery costs almost nothing in the regime where it is unnecessary, which is what makes it safe to deploy before you know you need it. Second, SHARP loses one seed of twelve. It is not magic; it is a favourable bet, and a paired \(t\)-test on post-warmup profit rejects the null decisively (\(t=6.7\), \(p<0.0001\)).

09
From one price to a portfolio of decisions

A single price is rarely the real object. You run a book of them — products, segments, markets — that share regime risk. Reflexivity changes portfolio construction, not just point decisions, because it changes the tail. We score a book by mean profit penalised by the CVaR of its post-break drawdown, and let each agent allocate an “aggressiveness budget” across three markets of increasing competitive ferocity.

PortfolioMean profitCVaR drawdownScore
Naive snapshot22461329918
SHARP29321472785

SHARP cuts the tail drawdown by roughly 9× and triples the risk-adjusted score. More interestingly, it reallocates: the “knife-fight” market that the naive book treats as its biggest liability becomes SHARP’s largest holding, because SHARP can actually defend it. Managing reflexivity is not only a per-decision fix; it is what lets you hold positions a snapshot-based book has to avoid.

10
Does this happen in the real world? A pharma case

Everything so far is a simulation faithful to a mechanism. Fair to ask: does the mechanism actually fire outside a notebook? In competitive branded pharmaceuticals it fires on a schedule, and twice recently in plain view.

A value-based price is correct for a market with a passive rival. It is a starting gun for one with an active rival.

PCSK9 inhibitors (2018–19): a public tit-for-tat

Amgen’s Repatha and Sanofi/Regeneron’s Praluent — two cholesterol biologics — both launched near $14,000/year in 2015, each priced against cost-effectiveness thresholds in isolation. Payers balked, uptake stalled. In late 2018 Amgen cut Repatha’s list price roughly 60% to $5,850; months later Regeneron and Sanofi matched Praluent to the identical $5,850 — a rare public tit-for-tat on pre-rebate list prices. The correct value-based estimate was correct for the equilibrium it measured and wrong for the one it helped create. Sanofi ultimately exited US cardiovascular altogether.

GLP-1 agonists (2025–26): matched in weeks

In the obesity/diabetes duopoly of Novo Nordisk (Wegovy/Ozempic) and Eli Lilly (Zepbound/Mounjaro), Novo cut cash prices from $499 to $349 a month; within weeks Lilly best-responded with further LillyDirect cuts, and Novo subsequently slashed list prices 35–50% to $675. Cross-brand share moved materially over the same window. The response latency is measured in weeks — faster than most teams re-estimate.

Why pharma is a sharper instance, not a weaker one

Pharma adds structure that makes the reflexive framing more apt. The decision surface is the net price and formulary access, mediated by pharmacy benefit managers — and list-price moves are often around the pre-existing net price, so naïve list-price elasticity is doubly misleading. Exogenous policy regime shifts (Medicare negotiation under the Inflation Reduction Act; government direct-purchase deals) coincide with competitive activation — exactly the regime-change signal SHARP’s detector is built to catch. And payer/rebate uncertainty is a natural ambiguity set for the distributionally-robust mode. The SHARP objects map cleanly: two-sided instruments become own- and rival-net-price shifters (manufacturing cost, exogenous policy timing); regime detection fires on rival activation or a policy shock; DRO prices under payer ambiguity when the instrument for net demand weakens.

Scope, honestly. This applies to competitive branded classes with two or more rivals contesting access. It does not apply during patent-protected monopoly, where there is no strategic responder and an ordinary causal estimate suffices. The value shows up precisely when a class becomes contested — at biosimilar entry, at a second-in-class launch, or when a rival turns price into a weapon.

11
Run it yourself

The framework is a small, dependency-light Python package. Every figure and number above is regenerated from the same canonical config, so nothing here is hand-drawn.

# install
pip install -e .

# the core result in ~20 lines
python examples/demo_headtohead.py

# the two diagnostics as a function of competitor aggression
python examples/demo_reflexivity_gap.py

# reflexivity changes portfolio construction
python examples/demo_portfolio.py

# the test suite (claims as executable checks)
pytest -q

The constructive framework — five moves — maps directly onto the modules:

  • Estimate response functions, not elasticities. Model \(P(\text{competitor response}\mid \text{my price, state})\). competitor.py
  • Instrument both sides of the market. Two instruments \((z_a, z_c)\) for two endogenous prices, via cross-fitted DML + 2SLS. estimator.py
  • Make regime detection a first-class citizen. CUSUM, Chow, first-stage-F monitoring on every window. regime.py
  • Treat the causal estimate as a snapshot with an expiry date. Rolling re-estimation triggered by alarms and expiry events. controller.py
  • Shift from “what does price do?” to “what game am I playing?” Stackelberg and distributionally-robust objectives. optimizer.py, reflexive.py

12
Honest novelty & limits

SHARP does not invent its ingredients; it composes known ones into a decision loop and adds two decision objects. The intellectual lineage is explicit. The Lucas Critique (1976) is the diagnosis. Performative prediction (Perdomo et al., 2020) formalises models that change the distribution they predict, and its performative-optimum / stable-point distinction is the direct ancestor of the RCE. That field has since grown quickly: the multi-agent branch already studies strategic responders in decision-dependent games (Narang et al., 2023), and even the transition from stability to chaos under repeated retraining (Piliouras & Yu, 2023) — which is precisely the ρ_R < 1 versus ρ_R ≥ 1 boundary, arrived at from a different direction. So the strategic angle is not what is new here. What is new is the lens: SHARP puts an identified causal estimate at the centre, so performativity stops being a generic distribution shift and becomes the failure of an exclusion restriction — an identification problem. That reframing is what lets the same instruments that identified the effect also diagnose when it has expired. On top of that sit the practical pieces: the Reflexivity Gap \(\mathcal{G}\) as a decision-facing cost, the reflexivity spectral radius \(\rho_R\) as an estimable ship/hedge rule, an agent that switches its own objective, and a real domain (pricing) where the loop demonstrably fires. Double/debiased ML and 2SLS supply the identification; distributionally robust optimisation supplies the hedge.

The limits are real. The competitor model is a parametric best-response with a single gain and latency; real rivals are messier and may themselves be learning, which raises the genuinely open question of two adaptive agents co-moving. The demand system is constant-elasticity; richer functional forms change the optimiser but not the operator logic. The spectral radius is estimated by finite differences and inherits their noise. And the benchmark is a simulation — faithful to the mechanism, but a simulation. Treat the numbers as a demonstration that the mechanism behaves as the theory predicts, not as a promise about your market.

The goal is not a causal estimate that never goes stale. It is an agent that knows the expiry date of its own beliefs — and prices the game it is actually in.

If you work on pricing, performativity, strategic ML, or causal decision systems and want to push on the two-adaptive-agents case or test the operator on real data, I would like to hear from you.

SHARP · Strategic-Hedged Adaptive Re-estimation for Pricing

The formalisation (Reflexive Operator, RCE, Reflexivity Gap, reflexivity spectral radius), the Python framework, the agentic controller, and all experiments in this piece are released together. Every figure above is generated from the accompanying code.

Framework, paper, and reproducible benchmark released together. Written to invite collaboration, not to close a question.
